## From sparse counts to dense meaning

Bag-of-words methods represent text with very long sparse vectors. That is often
good enough for classification or similarity, but it treats words as unrelated
labels. `Word embeddings` change that by representing each word as a short dense
vector in which proximity reflects shared usage.

The guiding idea is distributional: words that appear in similar contexts tend to
have related meanings. In the old phrase often attributed to Firth, you know a word
by the company it keeps.

## What embeddings try to capture

A good embedding space lets us say more than "this word appeared often." It lets us
compare usage patterns directly.

- Words with similar contexts should be close in the vector space.
- Directions in the space may capture recurring semantic contrasts.
- Dense vectors can be used as features for later models.

This is why analogies like `king - man + woman ≈ queen` became famous. The analogy
is not magic; it is an illustration of the fact that some regularities in word use
can become approximately linear in the learned space.

## Co-occurrence and PMI give one route to embeddings

One route starts from a co-occurrence matrix rather than a document-term matrix.
Instead of counting how often a word appears in a document, we count how often a
target word appears near a context word inside a fixed window.

If `Co_{ij}` is the co-occurrence count for target word `i` and context word `j`,
then the normalized joint and marginal probabilities are:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{Co_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} Co_{ij}},
\qquad
p_i = \frac{\sum_j Co_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} Co_{ij}},
\qquad
p_j = \frac{\sum_i Co_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} Co_{ij}}
$$

Pointwise Mutual Information compares the observed joint frequency with what we
would expect under independence:

$$
\operatorname{PMI}(i,j) = \log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{p_i p_j}\right)
$$

High PMI means the pair occurs together more often than chance would suggest.

## PPMI and SVD turn co-occurrence into dense vectors

Raw PMI has two practical problems. It can be negative, and it often overweights
rare events. A common fix is positive PMI:

$$
\operatorname{PPMI}(i,j) = \max\!\left(\log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{p_i p_j}\right), 0\right)
$$

The resulting matrix is still large and sparse, so we reduce it with truncated
Singular Value Decomposition:

$$
M \approx U_K S_K V_K'
$$

The rows of the reduced factor matrix give dense word vectors. This keeps the
distributional idea while compressing the vocabulary into a lower-dimensional space
that is easier to compare with cosine similarity.

## Word2Vec uses prediction instead of matrix factorization

The better-known route is predictive. `Word2Vec` popularized shallow neural methods
that learn embeddings by solving self-supervised prediction tasks on running text.

Two standard variants are:

- **CBOW**: predict the center word from surrounding context words.
- **Skip-gram**: predict surrounding context words from the center word.

The legacy `GRA 4164` slides focus on skip-gram. Slide a context window across the
corpus, create target-context pairs, and train a shallow network so words that
share contexts end up with similar hidden-layer vectors.

::: {.teacher-only}
Emphasize that the hidden layer is the real prize. The prediction task is only the
training mechanism used to learn useful word vectors.
:::

## Negative sampling makes training practical

A full softmax over a large vocabulary is expensive. `Negative sampling` reduces the
cost by pairing each observed target-context relation with a small number of random
non-context words. The model is then trained to score real neighbours highly and
sampled noise words poorly.

This changes the computation, but not the intuition: useful embeddings are learned
when the model can distinguish real contextual neighbours from implausible ones.

## Why embeddings matter and where they break

Word embeddings improved on bag-of-words in three important ways:

- they are dense rather than sparse,
- they support similarity and analogy operations directly,
- and they can be fed into downstream models as reusable features.

But classic Word2Vec-style embeddings are still static. A word gets one vector no
matter which sentence it appears in. That is a real limitation for polysemous words
such as `apple`, `bank`, or `charge`.

This is one reason later contextual models such as BERT and transformer-based LLMs
became so important: they produce token representations that depend on the sentence
around them.

## What to report

When students present an embeddings-based analysis in this course, they should
report:

- what context definition was used, including the window size,
- whether the approach was matrix-factorization-based or predictive,
- how similarity between words was measured,
- one interpretable nearest-neighbour or analogy result, and
- one honest limitation, especially around static meaning or sensitivity to corpus
  choice.
